---
title: "<img src='hex.svg' width='200px'/><br>medicalcoder:"
subtitle: "A Unified and Longitudinally Aware Framework<br>for ICD-Based Comorbidity Assessment in R"
author:
  - name: Peter E. DeWitt, PhD
    affiliation:
      - <small> Department of Biomedical Informatics, University of Colorado School of Medicine, University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus, Aurora, Colorado, USA</small>
format:
  revealjs:
    theme:
      - default
      - slides.scss
    logo: hex.svg
    fontsize: 2em
    html-math-method: mathjax
    slide-number: c
    show-slide-number: all
    highlight-style: rstudio_default-light.theme
embed-resources: true   # true = a single file, false = multiple files
knitr:
  opts_chunk:      ########## set global options ############
    collapse: true # keep code from blocks together (if shown)
    echo: false    # don't show code
    message: true  # show messages
    warning: true  # show warnings
    error: true    # show error messages
    cache: true
    R.options:
      digits: 3    # round to three digits
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
editor: source
highlight-style: rstudio_default-light.theme
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| cache: true
library(data.table)
library(medicalcoder)
options("datatable.print.topn" = 3)
mimiciv <- arrow::read_feather("../mimiciv.feather")
data.table::setDT(mimiciv)
```
```{r}
#| label: charlson-comorbidities-call-current
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "setup"
charlson_encounterlevel_quan2005 <-
  medicalcoder::comorbidities(
    data        = mimiciv,
    icd.codes   = "icd_code",
    method      = "charlson_quan2005",
    id.vars     = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
    icdv.var    = "icd_version",
    dx.var      = "dx",
    poa         = 1,
    age.var     = "age",
    primarydx   = 0,
    flag.method = "current" # Default
  )
```
```{r}
#| label: charlson-comorbidities-call-cumulative
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "setup"
charlson_longitudinal_quan2005 <-
  medicalcoder::comorbidities(
    data        = mimiciv,
    icd.codes   = "icd_code",
    method      = "charlson_quan2005",
    id.vars     = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
    icdv.var    = "icd_version",
    dx.var      = "dx",
    poa         = 1,
    age.var     = "age",
    primarydx   = 0,
    flag.method = "cumulative"
  )
```
```{r}
#| label: elixhauser-quan2005-current
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "setup"
elixhauser_encounterlevel_quan2005 <-
  medicalcoder::comorbidities(
    data        = mimiciv,
    icd.codes   = "icd_code",
    method      = "elixhauser_quan2005",
    id.vars     = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
    icdv.var    = "icd_version",
    dx.var      = "dx",
    poa         = 1,
    primarydx   = 0,
    flag.method = "current"
  )
```
```{r}
#| label: elixhauser-quan2005-cumulative
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "setup"
elixhauser_longitudinal_quan2005 <-
  medicalcoder::comorbidities(
    data        = mimiciv,
    icd.codes   = "icd_code",
    method      = "elixhauser_quan2005",
    id.vars     = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
    icdv.var    = "icd_version",
    dx.var      = "dx",
    poa         = 1,
    primarydx   = 0,
    flag.method = "cumulative"
  )
```
```{r}
#| label: cdelta
#| echo: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: ["charlson-comorbidities-call-current", "charlson-comorbidities-call-cumulative", "elixhauser-quan2005"]
cdelta <-
  data.table::rbindlist(
    list(
      "Charlson__Encounter-level"   = charlson_encounterlevel_quan2005,
      "Charlson__Longitudinal"      = charlson_longitudinal_quan2005,
      "Elixhauser__Encounter-level" = elixhauser_encounterlevel_quan2005,
      "Elixhauser__Longitudinal"    = elixhauser_longitudinal_quan2005
    ),
    idcol = "set",
    use.names = TRUE,
    fill = TRUE
  )
cdelta[, c("method", "flag.method") := data.table::tstrsplit(set, "__")]
cdelta[, set := NULL]
```
```{r}
#| label: cdelta-long
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "cdelta"
cdelta_long <-
  data.table::melt(
    cdelta,
    id.vars = c("method", "flag.method", "subject_id", "encounter"),
    na.rm = TRUE
  )
cdelta_long <- cdelta_long[!(variable %in% c("age_score", "num_cmrb", "cci", "cmrb_flag", "readmission_index", "mortality_index", "HTN_C"))]
cdelta_long <- cdelta_long[, .(count = sum(value)), by = .(method, flag.method, variable)]
```
```{r}
#| label: ahrq2026current
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "setup"
ahrq2026current <-
  medicalcoder::comorbidities(
    data          = mimiciv,
    icd.codes     = "icd_code",
    id.vars       = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
    icdv.var      = "icd_version",
    dx.var        = "dx",
    poa.var       = "poa",
    primarydx.var = "pdx",
    method        = "elixhauser_ahrq2026",
    flag.method   = "current" # Encounter-level
  )
```
```{r}
#| label: ahrq2026cumulative
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "setup"
ahrq2026cumulative <-
  medicalcoder::comorbidities(
    data          = mimiciv,
    icd.codes     = "icd_code",
    id.vars       = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
    icdv.var      = "icd_version",
    dx.var        = "dx",
    poa.var       = "poa",
    primarydx.var = "pdx",
    method        = "elixhauser_ahrq2026",
    flag.method   = "cumulative" # Longitudinal, sort within subject_id by encounter
  )
```
```{r}
#| label: ahrq2026delta
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: ["ahrq2026current", "ahrq2026cumulative"]
ahrq2026delta <-
  merge(
    x = ahrq2026current,
    y = ahrq2026cumulative,
    all = TRUE,
    by = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
    suffixes = c("_current", "_cumulative")
  )
```


## 

* Have you ever:
  * Worked with ICD-9 and ICD-10 data?
  * Assessed Charlson or Elixhauser comorbidities?
  * Assessed Pediatric Complex Chronic Conditions (PCCC)?
  * Been concerned about present-on-admission flags?
  * Primary verse secondary diagnosis?

  * Needed a __longitudinal__ patient context?
    * 76.4% of US adults report at least one chronic condition
    * 59.5% report two or more

_medicalcoder_ supports all of this natively!

::: {style="font-size: 0.5em;"}

---

Watson, Kathleen B., et al. "Trends in multiple chronic conditions among US
adults, by life stage, behavioral risk factor surveillance system, 2013–2023."
Preventing chronic disease 22 (2025): E15.

:::

::: {.notes}

I want to start with a few questions that may sound familiar.

Have you had to work with ICD-9 or ICD-10 codes in your datasets?

Have you had to map those codes to comorbidity systems such as Charlson,
Elixhauser, or pediatric complex chronic conditions (PCCC)?

Have you had to worry about present-on-admission flags? primary versus secondary
diagnoses? mixed ICD versions? 

Or, most importantly for this presentation, the longitudinal nature of patient
records?

If so, then you have probably run into the same frustrations that I have. 
There are available tools but they are fragmented. 

One tool gives you Charlson, another gives you Elixhauser, a third gives you
PCCC. They may be written in different languages, require different input
structures, and handle mixed ICD-9 and ICD-10 data inconsistently. In practice,
it is messy.

I built `medicalcoder` to relieve the frustrations and make this type of work
easier and more consistent.

`medicalcoder` is designed for real-world EHR data. It handles mixed ICD
versions within the same dataset, full and compact code formats,
present-on-admission status, primary diagnosis indicators, and multiple
comorbidity algorithms. 

Most importantly, it can account for longitudinal patient history.

Why does that matter?

Because chronic conditions are common (3 out of 4 adults in the US reporting at
least one chronic condition), and they are not documented on every encounter. I
know that from the literature, and I know it from my own medical records. If I
look only at encounter-level diagnosis codes, I will miss chronic conditions
that are still clinically relevant but simply were not recorded at that visit.

:::

## Example Data: MIMIC-IV v3.1

```{r}
#| label: show-mimiciv-data
#| echo: true
#| dependson: "setup"
mimiciv
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.5em;"}

---

Get access to the MIMIC-IV v3.1 data: https://physionet.org/content/mimiciv/3.1/

:::

::: {.notes}

To illustrate that point, I will use the MIMIC-IV dataset. If you are not
familiar with it, MIMIC-IV is a de-identified clinical dataset of adult patients
from Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center. It is especially useful here because
it includes multiple encounters per patient and contains both ICD-9 and ICD-10
codes.

For the following examples, I prepared the data in long format, with one ICD
code per row and additional variables indicating code version, diagnosis or
procedure status, present-on-admission status, and primary diagnosis where
relevant, and of course, subject and encounter ids.

You can gain access to this data through physionet.  The code I used to prepare
the data is available on GitHub.  The URL is on the final slide of this
presentation.

Notes on the columns (if needed)

| Column         | Description                                        | Source                |
| :--            | :------                                            | :--                   |
| `subject_id`   | subject identifier                                 | MIMIC-IV              |
| `encounter`    | Encounter Sequence within `subject_id`             | constructed           |
| `age`          | Age, in years, at admission                        | MIMIC-IV; constructed |
| `icd_code`     | ICD code                                           | MIMIC-IV              |
| `icd_version ` | ICD version integer value 9 or 10                  | MIMIC-IV              |
| `dx`           | Indicator for diagnostic (1) or procedure (0) code | MIMIC-IV; constructed |
| `poa`          | Present-on-admission flag                          | constructed           |
| `pdx`          | Primary diagnosis flag                             | constructed           |

:::

## Example Data: MIMIC-IV v3.1

```{r}
#| label: mixed-icd-in-mimiciv
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
#| dependson: "setup"

# how many subjects have both ICD-9 and ICD-10 codes?
s <-
  mimiciv[
    ,
    .(
      icdv =
        data.table::fcase(
          all(is.na(icd_version)),               "No ICD codes",
          all(icd_version ==  9L, na.rm = TRUE), "ICD-9 Only",
          all(icd_version == 10L, na.rm = TRUE), "ICD-10 Only",
          any(icd_version ==  9L, na.rm = TRUE) & any(icd_version == 10L, na.rm = TRUE), "Both ICD-9 and ICD-10"
        )
    )
    ,
    by = .(subject_id)
   ][
   , .(subjects = data.table::uniqueN(subject_id)), keyby = .(icdv)
   ][
   , sp := sprintf("%0.1f%%", 100 * subjects / sum(subjects))
   ][
   , s := qwraps2::frmt(subjects)
   ]

# how many encounters have both ICD-9 and ICD-10 codes?
e <-
  mimiciv[
    ,
    .(
      icdv =
        data.table::fcase(
          all(is.na(icd_version)),               "No ICD codes",
          all(icd_version ==  9L, na.rm = TRUE), "ICD-9 Only",
          all(icd_version == 10L, na.rm = TRUE), "ICD-10 Only",
          any(icd_version ==  9L, na.rm = TRUE) & any(icd_version == 10L, na.rm = TRUE), "Both ICD-9 and ICD-10"
        )
    )
    ,
    by = .(subject_id, encounter)
   ][
   , .(encounters = data.table::uniqueN(.SD, by = c("subject_id", "encounter"))), keyby = .(icdv)
   ][
   , ep := sprintf("%0.3f%%", 100 * encounters / sum(encounters))
   ][
   , e := qwraps2::frmt(encounters)
   ]

tab <-
  rbind(
    merge(s, e, by = "icdv")[, .(icdv, s, sp, e, ep)],
    data.table::data.table(
      icdv = "Totals:",
      "s" = qwraps2::frmt(data.table::uniqueN(mimiciv, by = c("subject_id"))),
      "sp" = "",
      "e" = qwraps2::frmt(data.table::uniqueN(mimiciv, by = c("subject_id", "encounter"))),
      "ep" = ""
    )
  )

kableExtra::kbl(
  x = tab,
  format = "html",
  col.names = c("", "N", "%", "N", "%"),
  align = "lrrrr"
) |>
kableExtra::kable_styling(
  bootstrap_options = c("striped")
) |>
kableExtra::add_header_above(c("", "Subjects" = 2, "Encounters" = 2)) |>
kableExtra::row_spec(5, bold = TRUE)
```

`r qwraps2::frmt(nrow(mimiciv))` total rows

::: {.notes}

One point worth emphasizing is that mixed ICD data are not rare. In MIMIC-IV,
tens of thousands of subjects have both ICD-9 and ICD-10 codes, and even some
encounters contain both. Any workflow that assumes a single ICD version
throughout a dataset is going to lose information or require error-prone post
hoc aggregation.

medicalcoder was built to handle that directly.

:::

## Encounter-level vs Longitudinal Flagging of Comorbidities

```{r}
#| label: charlson-elixhauser-at-least-one-condidtion-graphic
#| echo: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: ["cdelta"]
# encounters with at least one comorbidity
cdelta[, .(n = sum(cmrb_flag), p = mean(cmrb_flag)), by = .(method, flag.method)] |>
  ggplot2::ggplot() +
  ggplot2::aes(x = method, fill = flag.method, y = n) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(position = ggplot2::position_dodge()) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Number of Encounters with at least one comorbidity",
    label = scales::label_comma(),
    sec.axis = ggplot2::sec_axis(
      name = "Percent of Encounters at least one comorbidity",
      transform = ~ . / nrow(unique(mimiciv, by = c("subject_id", "encounter"))),
      breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1),
      label = scales::label_percent()
    )
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Flag Method",
    values = c("Encounter-level" = "#6F263D", "Longitudinal" = "#4F8FCB")
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw() +
  ggplot2::theme(
    axis.title.x = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.5em;"}

---

Quan, Hude, et al. "Coding algorithms for defining comorbidities in ICD-9-CM and
ICD-10 administrative data." Medical care 43.11 (2005): 1130-1139. DOI:
10.1097/01.mlr.0000182534.19832.83

:::

::: {.notes}

Now let us look at results from two common comorbidity systems: Charlson and
Elixhauser, using the Quan 2005 mappings.

A basic but important observation is that diagnosis codes associated with a
comorbidity are not reported on every encounter. The condition may still be
present even when the code is absent. So if we allow comorbidities to persist
longitudinally once established, we identify more encounters in which the
condition is relevant.

That idea is easy to understand in the abstract, but it becomes more interesting
when severity changes over time. Diabetes is a good example.

:::


## Diabetes

```{r}
#| label: dmplot
#| echo: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "cdelta"
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6
g1 <-
  ggplot2::ggplot(
  cdelta_long[variable %in% c("dm", "dmc", "DM", "DMCX")][,
    .(flag.method, method, count,
      variable = data.table::fifelse(variable %in% c("dm", "DM"),
        "Diabetes\nwithout complications", "Diabetes\nwith complications"))]
  ) +
  ggplot2::aes(x = variable, fill = flag.method, y = count) +#, color = variable) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(linewidth = 3, position = ggplot2::position_dodge()) +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap( ~ method) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Number of Encounters",
    label = scales::label_comma()
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Flag Method",
    values = c("Encounter-level" = "#6F263D", "Longitudinal" = "#4F8FCB")
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw() +
  ggplot2::theme(
    axis.title.x = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

s10009326 <-
  data.table::melt(
    cdelta[subject_id == "10009326"],
    id.vars = c("encounter", "method", "flag.method"),
    measure.vars = c("dm", "dmc", "DM", "DMCX"),
    variable.factor = FALSE
  )

s10009326[, variable := data.table::fifelse(variable %in% c("dm", "DM"),
  "Diabetes\nwithout\nComplications", "Diabetes\nwith\nComplications")]

s10009326 <- s10009326[value == 1]

g2 <-
  ggplot2::ggplot(s10009326) +
  ggplot2::aes(x = encounter, y = flag.method, fill = flag.method) +
  ggplot2::geom_tile(color = "white") +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, max(s10009326$encounter), by=1)) +
  ggh4x::facet_nested(method + variable ~ ., scales = "free_y")  +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Flag Method",
    values = c("Encounter-level" = "#6F263D", "Longitudinal" = "#4F8FCB")
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_bw() +
  ggplot2::theme(
    axis.text.y = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    axis.title.y = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    legend.title = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  ) +
  ggplot2::ggtitle("subject id 10009326")

ggpubr::ggarrange(g1, g2, nrow = 1, common.legend = TRUE, legend = "bottom")
```

::: {.notes}

Under both Charlson and Elixhauser, diabetes can be classified as either without
complications or with complications. 

For one particular subject,
there are encounters where no diabetes code appears at all, even though the
patient clearly still has diabetes (encounters 2, 5, and 9).

If we rely only on encounter-level coding, those encounters look falsely clean.

Once we account for patient history, we carry the condition forward. More
importantly, we can also carry forward the most severe established state when
that is what the algorithm requires. In other words, a longitudinal approach
changes not only whether diabetes is flagged, but also how it is classified.

This is seen in the aggregate as there are fewer encounters flagged as DM without
complications when accounting for patient history compared to the results when
only looking within an encounter.

This matters for cohort definition and risk adjustment.

Now, someone could reasonably say: if the only issue is persistence, then maybe
we can just flag comorbidities at the encounter level and carry them forward
later. Sometimes that works. But there are more nuanced cases where
important information is lost before you ever get the chance to do that.

| Method      | Encounter-level                  | Longitudinal            |
|:--          | :---:                            | :---:                   |
| Charlson    | 12/17 w/o cx; 1/17 w cx; 4 no dm | 11/17 w/o cx; 6/17 w cx |
| Elixhauser  | 11/17 w/o cx; 2/17 w cx; 4 no dm | 5/17 w/o cx; 12/17 w cx |

:::

## Elixhauser per AHRQ

* [Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality](https://www.ahrq.gov/) (AHRQ)
* Present-on-admission is _required_ for _some_ conditions/ICD codes
* Example:
  * K76.0: Fatty (change of) liver, not elsewhere classified
  * Maps to mild liver disease
  * Must be present-on-admission to flag the comorbidity

::: {.notes}

One example comes from the AHRQ version of Elixhauser, where some conditions
depend on present-on-admission status. That distinction matters because it
separates comorbidities the patient came in with from complications or acute
issues that arose during the encounter.

Cancer, is present-on-admission exempt. Cancer is flagged on the encounter the
ICD code is reported on regardless of present-on-admission status.

Mild liver disease, on the other hand, requires the ICD code to be flagged as
present-on-admission. 

A specific example: K76.0 Fatty (change of) Liver not elsewhere classified,
maps to mild liver disease and requires POA to be flagged.

:::

## Subject 19997538:

```{r}
#| label: show-s19997538-liver-disease
#| echo: true
#| output: true
mimiciv[subject_id == 19997538, .(icdcodes = .N, encounters = max(encounter))]
mimiciv[subject_id == 19997538 & icd_code == "K760"]
```

```{r}
#| label: K760-get-icd-codes
#| include: false
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: get_icd_codes()
subset(
  medicalcoder::get_icd_codes(with.description = TRUE),
  code == "K760"
)
```

```{r}
#| label: K760-get-elixhauser-codes()
#| include: false
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: get_elixhauser_codes()
subset(
  x      = medicalcoder::get_elixhauser_codes(),
  subset = code == "K760" & elixhauser_ahrq2026 == 1L,
  select = c("icdv", "dx", "full_code", "code", "poaexempt", "condition", "elixhauser_ahrq2026")
)
```

* Results from `medicalcoder`

```{r}
#| label: show-s19997538-liver-disease-part-2
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
#| cache: true
#| dependson: ["ahrq2026current", "ahrq2026cumulative"]
keep <- c("subject_id", "encounter", "LIVER_MLD")
merge(
  x = ahrq2026current[subject_id == 19997538, .SD, .SDcols = keep],
  y = ahrq2026cumulative[subject_id == 19997538, .SD, .SDcols = keep],
  all =  TRUE,
  by = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
  suffixes = c("  Encounter-level", "  Longitudinal")
) |>
gt::gt()
```

* Simple encounter level carry-forward will fail to flag the mild liver disease

::: {.notes}

For this subject K760 appears on encounter 1 of 3, and is reported as
not-present-on-admission.  

(NOTE: POA flags are not part of the MIMIC-IV data, I constructed these flags
probabilistically based on sequence number)

When flagging comorbidities using only encounter level data, the mild liver
disease is correctly _not_ flagged on encounter 1.  It is also not flagged on
encounters 2 and 3 because the relevent code was not reported on the subsequent
encounters.

When considering the longitudinal nature of medical records, the condition is
correctly not flagged on encounter 1 and is flagged on encounters 2 and 3
because of the patient's history.

Once the history of a condition exists, it can become
relevant for future encounters even when the code is not re-reported.

If you only compute encounter-level flags and then try to repair things later,
you cannot recover information that was never retained in the first place. The
longitudinal logic has to be part of the flagging process itself.

:::

## PCCC

* ICD codes map to conditions and subconditions
* Technology dependence is a subcondition
* Flag conditions due to technology dependence only if at least one
  non-technology dependence code also appears in the record

::: {style="font-size: 0.5em;"}

---

Feinstein, James A., et al. "Pediatric complex chronic condition system version
3." JAMA Network Open 7.7 (2024): e2420579.
doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2024.20579

:::


::: {.notes}

The same problem becomes even more pronounced in more complex systems such as
PCCC. There, condition assignment can depend on combinations of diagnosis and
procedure information, including distinctions such as technology dependence. In
those settings, encounter-only logic can both miss conditions and misclassify
their severity. Carrying forward the right historical information can change the
patient's risk profile in meaningful ways.

:::


## Subject 10728333

```{r}
#| label: pccc-current
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "setup"
medicalcoder_pccc_v3.1_current <-
  medicalcoder::comorbidities(
    data        = mimiciv,
    icd.codes   = "icd_code",
    id.vars     = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
    icdv.var    = "icd_version",
    dx.var      = "dx",
    poa         = 1L,
    method      = "pccc_v3.1",
    flag.method = "current"
  )
```
```{r}
#| label: pccc-cumulative
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "setup"
medicalcoder_pccc_v3.1_cumulative <-
  medicalcoder::comorbidities(
    data        = mimiciv,
    icd.codes   = "icd_code",
    id.vars     = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
    icdv.var    = "icd_version",
    dx.var      = "dx",
    poa         = 1L,
    method      = "pccc_v3.1",
    flag.method = "cumulative"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: s19997538
#| include: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: ["pccc-current", "pccc-cumulative"]
s10728333 <-
  merge(
    x = medicalcoder_pccc_v3.1_current[subject_id == 10728333],
    y = medicalcoder_pccc_v3.1_cumulative[subject_id == 10728333],
    all = TRUE,
    by = c("subject_id", "encounter"),
    suffixes = c("_current", "_cumulative")
 )
Filter(f = function(x) sum(x) > 0, s10728333)
# metabolic, respriatory, any_tech, num_cmrb, misc,
```

```{r}
#| label: s10728333-table
#| echo: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "s19997538"
DT0 <- s10728333[subject_id == 10728333, .SD, .SDcols = patterns("encounter|^(met|resp|misc|num_|any_tech)")]
DT1 <-
  DT0[
    ,
    .(encounter,
      metabolic__current = data.table::fcase(
        metabolic_dxpr_only_current == 1, "DxPr",
        metabolic_tech_only_current == 1, "Tech",
        metabolic_dxpr_and_tech_current == 1, "DxPrTech",
        default = ""),
      metabolic__cumulative = data.table::fcase(
        metabolic_dxpr_only_cumulative == 1, "DxPr",
        metabolic_tech_only_cumulative == 1, "Tech",
        metabolic_dxpr_and_tech_cumulative == 1, "DxPrTech",
        default = ""),
      misc__current = data.table::fcase(
        misc_dxpr_only_current == 1, "DxPr",
        misc_tech_only_current == 1, "Tech",
        misc_dxpr_and_tech_current == 1, "DxPrTech",
        default = ""),
      misc__cumulative = data.table::fcase(
        misc_dxpr_only_cumulative == 1, "DxPr",
        misc_tech_only_cumulative == 1, "Tech",
        misc_dxpr_and_tech_cumulative == 1, "DxPrTech",
        default = ""),
      respiratory__current = data.table::fcase(
        respiratory_dxpr_only_current == 1, "DxPr",
        respiratory_tech_only_current == 1, "Tech",
        respiratory_dxpr_and_tech_current == 1, "DxPrTech",
        default = ""),
      respiratory__cumulative = data.table::fcase(
        respiratory_dxpr_only_cumulative == 1, "DxPr",
        respiratory_tech_only_cumulative == 1, "Tech",
        respiratory_dxpr_and_tech_cumulative == 1, "DxPrTech",
        default = ""),
      any_tech_dep_current = data.table::fifelse(any_tech_dep_current == 1, "1", ""),
      any_tech_dep_cumulative = data.table::fifelse(any_tech_dep_cumulative == 1, "1", ""),
      num_cmrb_current = as.character(num_cmrb_current),
      num_cmrb_cumulative = as.character(num_cmrb_cumulative)
    )
  ]

ftm1 <- "*"
ftm2 <- "†"
DT1[encounter == 1, `:=`(respiratory__current = ftm1, respiratory__cumulative = ftm1)]
DT1[encounter %in% 2:3,`:=`(respiratory__cumulative = ftm2)]
DT1[encounter == 3, `:=`(misc__current = ftm1, misc__cumulative = ftm1)]

DT1 |>
  gt::gt(caption = "") |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "center") |>
  gt::tab_spanner("Metabolic", columns = c(metabolic__current, metabolic__cumulative)) |>
  gt::tab_spanner("Miscellaneous", columns = c(misc__current, misc__cumulative)) |>
  gt::tab_spanner("Respiratory", columns = c(respiratory__current, respiratory__cumulative)) |>
  gt::tab_spanner("Any Tech", columns = c(any_tech_dep_current, any_tech_dep_cumulative)) |>
  gt::tab_spanner("Comorbidity Count", columns = c(num_cmrb_current, num_cmrb_cumulative)) |>
  gt::cols_label(
    encounter = "Encounter",
    metabolic__current = "Current", metabolic__cumulative = "Cumulative",
    misc__current = "Current", misc__cumulative = "Cumulative",
    respiratory__current = "Current", respiratory__cumulative = "Cumulative",
    any_tech_dep_current = "Current", any_tech_dep_cumulative = "Cumulative",
    num_cmrb_current = "Current", num_cmrb_cumulative = "Cumulative"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_footnote(footnote = "* A technology-dependent code was reported on this encounter.  Since no non-technology-dependent code was reported no comorbidities are flagged.") |>
  gt::tab_footnote(footnote = "† A technology-dependent code has been carried forward in the record.  Since no non-technology-dependent code was reported on this, or a prior, encounter, no comorbidities are flagged.")
```

::: {.notes}

For this patient, quickly, we see that under an encounter-level only approach we
would never capture the miscellaneous condition (tech-dep or transplants) simply
becuase the tech-dependent code never occurs along with a non-tech-dependent
code --- Just like the POA issue from the prior example.

For respiratory, we have the data loss due to tech codes not
being carried forward on the encounter-level only resulting in either missing
the condition completely (encounters 4, and 5) or missclassifying the condition
(encounters 6 through 13)

The risk profile for this subject is very different depending on whether or not
we consider the whole patient history when flagging comorbidities.

:::

## The Point:

* Encounter-level flagging of comorbidities will

  * Under count conditions

  * Misclassify disease severity

  * Fail to flag some conditions entirely

* Carry-forward of an encounter-level flag is insufficient for flagging
  comorbidities in longitudinal data

::: {.notes}

So, that is the central point of this talk:

Encounter-level flagging is not enough.

It can undercount chronic conditions, misclassify severity, and fail entirely
for algorithms that depend on historical context or present-on-admission logic.

That is what `medicalcoder` is designed to address.

:::

## `medicalcoder::comorbidities()` {auto-animate=true}

::: {.notes}

From a user perspective, the package provides a unified interface --- Just one
function call.  `medicalcoder::comorbidities()`.

:::

## `medicalcoder::comorbidities()` {auto-animate=true}

```r
medicalcoder::comorbidities(
  ### Required inputs:
  data      = ,   # object inheriting data.frame; one ICD code per row
  icd.codes = ,   # character; name of column in data containing ICD codes
  method    = ,   # <family>_<variant>, e.g., "charlson_quan2005"
```

::: {.notes}
You are required to supply three items:

1. a data.frame, in a long format, one ICD-code per row
2. Specify which column in the data.frame contains the ICD codes
3. Specify the method, a comorbidity family and variant 
   * eg charlson_quan2005, charlson_quan2011, elixhauser_ahrq2026, pccc_v3.1, ...
   * Currently 17 options.

:::

## `medicalcoder::comorbidities()` {auto-animate=true}

```r
medicalcoder::comorbidities(
  ### Required inputs:
  data      = ,   # object inheriting data.frame; one ICD code per row
  icd.codes = ,   # character; name of column in data containing ICD codes
  method    = ,   # <family>_<variant>, e.g., "charlson_quan2005"

  ### Optional inputs controlling grouping and ICD code attributes:
  id.vars   = NULL,  # character; subject, encounter identifiers
  icdv.var  = NULL,  # character; column name for ICD version (9 or 10)
  icdv      = NULL,  # integer; constant ICD version for all rows
  dx.var    = NULL,  # character; column name indicating  diagnosis (1) or procedure (0)
  dx        = NULL,  # integer; constant dx indicator for all rows
  poa.var   = NULL,  # character; column name for present-on-admission indicator
  poa       = NULL,  # integer; constant POA indicator for all rows
  age.var   = NULL,  # character; column name for age in years (Charlson only)
  primarydx.var = NULL, # character; column name indicating primary diagnosis
  primarydx     = NULL, # integer; constant primary dx indicator
```

::: {.notes}

Optionally, but strongly encouraged,

You can also provide identifiers such as subject ID and encounter ID so the
package can evaluate records longitudinally in the proper order. 

For ICD version handling, you can either point to a variable in the data that
distinguishes ICD-9 from ICD-10 (icdv.var), or specify a constant if your
dataset uses only one version.  

That means the same interface works for mixed-version datasets as well as
simpler single-version cases.

Similar input paradigm for diagnostic/procedure codes, present-on-admission
(poa) flags, and primary diagnosis flags.

:::

## `medicalcoder::comorbidities()` {auto-animate=true}

```r
medicalcoder::comorbidities(
  ### Required inputs:
  data      = ,   # object inheriting data.frame; one ICD code per row
  icd.codes = ,   # character; name of column in data containing ICD codes
  method    = ,   # <family>_<variant>, e.g., "charlson_quan2005"

  ### Optional inputs controlling grouping and ICD code attributes:
  id.vars   = NULL,  # character; subject, encounter identifiers
  icdv.var  = NULL,  # character; column name for ICD version (9 or 10)
  icdv      = NULL,  # integer; constant ICD version for all rows
  dx.var    = NULL,  # character; column name indicating  diagnosis (1) or procedure (0)
  dx        = NULL,  # integer; constant dx indicator for all rows
  poa.var   = NULL,  # character; column name for present-on-admission indicator
  poa       = NULL,  # integer; constant POA indicator for all rows
  age.var   = NULL,  # character; column name for age in years (Charlson only)
  primarydx.var = NULL, # character; column name indicating primary diagnosis
  primarydx     = NULL, # integer; constant primary dx indicator

  ### Defaults:
  flag.method   = "current", # "current" (encounter-level) or "cumulative" (longitudinal)
  full.codes    = TRUE, # reference data[[icd.codes]] against full ICD codes, e.g., C4A.111
  compact.codes = TRUE, # reference data[[icd.codes]] against compact ICD codes, e.g., C4A111
  subconditions = FALSE # PCCC only: return subcondition flags when available
)
```

::: {.notes}

There are also defaults that control whether flagging is done at the current
encounter only or longitudinally, whether full or compact code formats are used,
and whether subconditions are retained where applicable.

:::

## Trusting the Results

* Testing
  * _medicalcoder_ results are equivalent to [MIMIC-IV Code](https://github.com/MIT-LCP/mimic-code)
  * Identical results to SAS code published by AHRQ
  * _medicalcoder_ vs other R packages
    * all observed differences have been false negatives from the other packages.
  * It's not a black box
    * All source materials and code are open for review:
    * [https://github.com/dewittpe/medicalcoder/](https://github.com/dewittpe/medicalcoder/)

::: {.notes}

Why should you trust the results?

First, the package is not a black box. The source code, internal mappings, and
algorithmic details are available on GitHub.

Second, I have validated the implementation against external references,
including code distributed with MIMIC, SAS code from AHRQ, and several other R
packages. 

During development I tracked down discrepancies, identified false
positives and false negatives, and corrected those issues. At this point I am
confident in the implementation and in the value of the longitudinal framework
it provides.

:::

## Depends on R &geq; 3.5.0, No Imports

```{r}
#| label: medicalcoder-packageDescription
#| echo: true
#| output: true
installed.packages()["medicalcoder", c("Depends", "Imports", "Suggests")]
```

* `data.table` and `dplyr` are suggested for performance

* All you need is a `medicalcoder_<version>.tar.gz` file and R &geq; 3.5.0

::: {.notes}

Installation is straightforward. The package works with R 3.5 or newer. Packages
such as `data.table` and `dplyr` are suggested for performance, but the dependency
footprint is intentionally light.

:::

## Takeaway

* Encounter-level flagging of comorbidities will

  * Under count conditions

  * Misclassify disease severity

  * Fail to flag some conditions entirely

* Carry-forward of an encounter-level flag is insufficient for flagging
  comorbidities in longitudinal data

* medicalcoder provides a light-weight, unified API allowing end users to
  quickly apply a comorbidity algorithm to a data set while accounting for
  present-on-admission status, primary diagnosis, ICD-version, ICD-type, full-
  and compact-code formats, and accounting for the longitudinal nature of
  medical data.

::: {.notes}
So to close, here are the takeaways.

Encounter-level comorbidity flagging is insufficient for many real analyses.

We need to account for the longitudinal nature of medical data.

A simple carry-forward of encounter-level flags is not enough in the more
complex cases.

`medicalcoder` provides a lightweight, unified R interface for applying
comorbidity algorithms while accounting for ICD version, code type,
present-on-admission status, primary diagnosis, full versus compact codes, and
patient history over time.
:::

## Thanks and Links

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width = "20%"}
![Peter E. DeWitt, PhD](dewitt.png)
:::
::: {.column width = "20%"}
![Seth Russell, MS](russell.png)
:::
::: {.column width = "20%"}
![Meg Rebull, MA](rebull.png)
:::
::: {.column width = "20%"}
![Jamie Feinstein, MD, MPH/MSPH](feinstein.jpg)
:::
::: {.column width = "20%"}
![Tell Bennett, MD, MS](bennett.png)
:::
::::

|                 |         |
|:-               |:------- |
| CRAN            | [https://cran.r-project.org/package=medicalcoder](https://cran.r-project.org/package=medicalcoder) |
| GitHub          | [https://github.com/dewittpe/medicalcoder](https://github.com/dewittpe/medicalcoder) |
| Package<br>Website | [http://www.peteredewitt.com/medicalcoder/](http://www.peteredewitt.com/medicalcoder/) |
| Slides          | [https://github.com/dewittpe/medicalcoder-R-medicine-2026](https://github.com/dewittpe/medicalcoder-R-medicine-2026) |

::: {.notes}

Thank you for your time.

I would also like to acknowledge the people who supported and contributed to
this work: Seth Russell, Meg Rebull, James Feinstein, and Tell Bennett.

The package site, CRAN page, GitHub repository, and slides are all linked in the
materials for this talk.

:::

# Appendix

## Conditions

```{r}
#| label: plot-conditions
#| echo: false
#| cache: true
#| dependson: "cdelta-long"
ggplot2::ggplot(cdelta_long) +
  ggplot2::aes(x = variable, fill = flag.method, y = count) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(position = ggplot2::position_dodge()) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Number of Encounters with at least one comorbidity",
    label = scales::label_comma(),
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Flag Method",
    values = c("Encounter-level" = "#6F263D", "Longitudinal" = "#4F8FCB")
  ) +
  ggplot2::coord_flip() +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap( ~ method, scales = "free") +
  ggplot2::theme_bw() +
  ggplot2::theme(
    axis.title.y = ggplot2::element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

<!-- ----------------------------------------------------------------------- -->
<!--                              End of File                                -->
<!-- ----------------------------------------------------------------------- -->
